risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were assigned to the two treatment groups according to a randomization list generated by the Biostatistics Department of MEDA Pharma. The random allocation was balanced using an undisclosed block size of six." Comment: This describes a generated randomization list and block randomization, which are sufficient context clues that a proper random sequence generation method was used.

Quote: "Patients were assigned to the two treatment groups according to a randomization list generated by the Biostatistics Department of MEDA Pharma. The random allocation was balanced using an undisclosed block size of six. The investigators and the monitor received sealed envelopes to enable decoding the individual blinded treatment in case of emergency." Comment: The report describes sequence generation and emergency code-break envelopes, but does not explicitly describe a concealment mechanism used at enrolment (e.g., central randomization or sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes for treatment assignment). 'Sealed envelopes' here refers to emergency unblinding and lacks the required details for allocation concealment before enrolment. Therefore allocation concealment is not clearly established.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The Neurological Assessment of Thioctic Acid in Diabetic Neuropathy (NATHAN) 1 trial was a multicenter ... randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled ... trial ... using film-coated tablets containing 600 mg ALA ... or matching placebo tablets with increased amounts of cellulose and lactose that were identical in appearance... The trial consisted of a ... 4-year double-blind phase." Comment: The report explicitly states that the trial was double-blind and used matching placebo tablets identical in appearance, which supports blinding of participants and treating personnel. Although some outcomes include symptoms/self-reported measures that could be influenced by lack of blinding, the article provides positive evidence that blinding was implemented with matching placebo and no evidence that blinding was broken; therefore performance bias is low risk overall.

Quote: "The Neurological Assessment of Thioctic Acid in Diabetic Neuropathy (NATHAN) 1 trial was a multicenter ... randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled..." and "Experienced, trained, and certified (by P.J.D. and colleagues) physicians evaluated the NIS and NSC... The nerve conduction, quantitative sensory tests (QSTs), and autonomic tests were performed by trained and certified personnel... All results were interactively evaluated by the Reading and Quality Assurance Centers." Comment: Outcome assessors were clinicians/trained personnel and central reading/quality centers, not patient self-report alone. The trial is explicitly described as double-blind, so assessors were intended to be blinded to allocation. Although some outcomes (e.g. NIS, NSC, TSS) are subjective/judgement-dependent, the explicit double-blind design indicates blinded outcome assessment, making detection bias unlikely.

Quote: "Clinical characteristics of the intention-to-treat population at baseline" with n = 230 (ALA) and 224 (Placebo) in Table 1; and in Table 2, at "4 Years," n = 215 (ALA) and 207 (Placebo). Quote: "The primary criterion of efficacy... [was] between baseline... and end point (mean of weeks 191 and 192 or last available value after randomization)." Comment: For the main 4-year efficacy outcome, 15/230 (6.5%) were not analysed in the ALA arm and 17/224 (7.6%) were not analysed in the placebo arm, an overall loss of about 7% with only ~1 percentage point imbalance between groups. This is essentially complete follow-up for the analysed outcome and does not suggest attrition bias. The paper also reports only 2 ALA vs 1 placebo discontinuations due to adverse events, with no evidence of substantial outcome-related or imbalanced exclusions.
